content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 6, 2017

Minh C. Tran
County Counsel
1195 Third Street, Suite 301
Napa, CA 94559

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-153

Dear Mr. Tran:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Napa County Planning Commissioner Michael Basayne regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

Must Commissioner Basayne, due to his employment in the wine tour industry, recuse himself from making, participating in making, or influencing the following Planning Commission decisions:

1. Decisions involving an existing winery's application to build a new facility?

2. Decisions affecting the wine industry, specifically decisions involving ordinances of general applicability such as the Winery Definition Ordinance (WDO)?

CONCLUSIONS

1. A decision to allow an existing winery to build an additional facility which will accommodate up to 320 visitors per week, provide wine tastings, and serving food and wine, would not appear to have a material financial effect on the value of the Commissioner's employer since the applicant is but one of 125 Napa wineries listed on the employer's website for tours.

_______________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No A-17-153
Page No. 2

2. Since 816 of the 2,196 businesses in unincorporated area of the county are wineries, this
constitutes a significant segment of the businesses in the jurisdiction (37 percent). While the
Commissioner's employer is not a winery, the employer is not uniquely affected by decisions
affecting the wine industry. Accordingly, the Commissioner can take part the decisions under the
"public generally" exception.

FACTS

Napa County is home to one of the world's premier wine industries. Napa County is famous
for its wines and the numerous wine-centered activities that in 2014 drew 3.3 million visitors to the
Napa Valley. The economic vigor of the wine industry is a vital part of the local economy. Napa
County remains a relatively small California county with an estimated population of approximately
142,166 residents.

In the late 1980s, the wine industry was a major part of agricultural growth of the Napa
Valley. After years of discussion between the wine industry and the local community, a WDO was
drafted. This ordinance went into effect in 1990. The WDO does the following:

1. Provides a definition of a "winery" for purposes of the WDO.

2. Allows for the production, the marketing and the sale of wine.

3. Sets prescribed limitations on all ancillary activities on the land other than wine production.

4. Permitted activities other than production are classified as "accessory" activities and can
only constitute less than 40% of the winery's activity.

5. There are also restrictions on grape sourcing.

6. The WDO can directly or indirectly affect the number of wineries permitted within the
county and how many visitors each winery can serve annually.

From time to time, the Napa County Planning Commission is charged with reviewing and or
amending Napa County's various zoning ordinances which include the WDO. The Planning
Commission consists of five Commissioners and a quorum is made up of at least three
Commissioners.

You provided a variety of statistics in your advice request. On June 30, 2017, you provided
clarifying data on the number of wineries and other business in Napa County. You stated that in the
unincorporated area of the county 816 of 2,196 businesses were wineries. This constituted 37
percent of the businesses in the unincorporated area of the county.

The Commissioner's Employer, Platypus Tours

• Commissioner Basayne is employed by Platypus Tours as the Director of Special Projects.
The Commissioner is a salaried employee with no ownership or investment interest in his

File No. A-17-153
Page No. 3

employer. Commissioner Basayne also does not receive any income from any winery, nor
does he receive any commission or compensation from any winery.

• Though Commissioner Basayne's job duties are not explicitly defined, his current duties
involve supporting the company through project management for constructing new offices,
as well as promoting the company as a tourist activity to other companies and entities
generally outside of Napa County and often outside of the State of California.

• Platypus Tours provides transportation services to individuals seeking to visit wineries in
Napa Valley and surrounding areas. Platypus Tours is paid directly by the individuals
purchasing its services. Additionally, Platypus Tours also books reservations at wineries and
pre-purchases tickets for wine tastings and other events when necessary. In the event that
Platypus Tours purchases wine tasting tickets for its customers, the full cost of the ticket is
billed to the individual customers.

• Platypus Tours offers two types of tours - pre-arranged "Join-In" tours for which individuals
and small groups can purchase tickets, and private tours that are specifically arranged at the
request and discretion of an individual or group of customers. In either instance, the
wineries visited may be selected through a combination of factors, including, but not limited
to, wineries requested by the tour participants, suggestions of the individual driver of the
tour, the ability of specific wineries to accommodate the group at that time, and the price
range selected. You stated that Platypus Tours website lists 125 Napa wineries.

• On rare occasions, a winery may purchase Platypus Tours' transportation services just as
any other group or individual would.

• To promote its services, Platypus Tours also cultivates substantial lists of wineries that are
among the many possibilities for customers to choose from when arranging to purchase
Platypus Tours' services. These lists are broken down on Platypus Tours' website by region,
and include 125 Napa County wineries and 98 Sonoma County wineries. At least some of
the wineries listed on Platypus Tours' website are currently in the process of obtaining
County approval for various projects - including expanding or opening wine tasting and
visitation - that will eventually be brought to the Planning Commission for approval.

The July 19, 2017 Application

On July 19, 2017, the Planning Commission will consider a winery application from a
business that currently operates an existing winery facility and that allows visitation and tastings
located within the unincorporated area of Napa County. You stated that the applicant is "one 125
Napa wineries included on the list on Platypus Tours' website." The application for consideration
would allow the winery to construct a new winery facility with up to 320 visitors per week for wine
tastings with food pairings. The new facility would be located approximately half a mile from the
existing winery facility.

File No. A-17-153
Page No. 4

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any interest enumerated in Section 87103. The interests pertinent to your
facts are:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in any source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) However,
Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a
business entity or real property, the potential conflict of interest is analyzed under
Regulation 18702.1 (relating to business interests) or Regulation 18702.2 (relating to real
property interests).

Mr Basayne has an interest in Platypus Tours pursuant to Section 87103(c) and (d). Under
your facts, however, he does not have a financial interest in any of the wineries.

Thus, if a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Platypus,
the Commissioner will have a conflict of interest (Section 87103.)

1. The July 19, 2017 Application

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision or not. Regulation 18701(a) provides:

"A financial effect ... is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision
before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a
proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real
property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6)."

The decision you described does not explicitly involve the Platypus Tours. Regulation
18701(b) provides that for all other cases, if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot

File No. A-17-153
Page No. 5

be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.

The standard to determine materiality for a business entity is set forth in Regulation
18702.1.² As applicable to your facts, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides:

"[T]he financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient
information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial
effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly
traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity."

A decision to allow an existing winery to build a new tasting food facility would not appear
to have a material effect on the value of Platypus.³ You stated that the applicant is but "one 125
Napa wineries included on the list on Platypus Tours' website." Thus, expanding one existing
facility would not likely have any effect on the value of the Platypus Tours.

2. WDO Decisions

While you have not presented a specific decision for our consideration, it would appear that
WDO decisions could potentially impact the entire wine industry in the County, as well as
indirectly impact Platypus Tours. However, as you identified in your letter, the Act prohibits an
official from making a decision only if the decision's effect on the official's interest is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Commonly referred to as
the "public generally" exception. Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general test:

"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his
or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant
segment."

Therefore, in order to invoke the exception, the official must establish that a significant
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her interest is not unique compared to the
effect on the significant segment. "Jurisdiction" is defined in Regulation 18703(d) as the
jurisdiction of the state or local government agency as defined in Section 82035, or the designated
geographical area the official was elected to represent, or the area to which the official's authority
and duties are limited if not elected. For Planning Commissioners, the jurisdiction at issue is the
unincorporated areas of Napa County.

Regulation 18703(b) further provides that a "significant segment" of the public is at least 25
percent of:

² Where the source of income is a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 refers back to the standard of applied in
18702.1 for business entities.

³ Regulation 18703(b) provides: "Notwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect
of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant."

File No. A-17-153
Page No. 6

"(1) All businesses or non-profit entities within the official's jurisdiction;

"(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction; or

"(3) All individuals within the official's jurisdiction."

You stated that 816 of 2,196 businesses in unincorporated area of the county are wineries. This constitutes a significant segment of the jurisdiction (37 percent). While the Commissioner's employer is not a winery, a decision affecting the wine industry would not have a unique effect on the employer as any effect on the employer is more attenuated than an effect on the wineries. Therefore, we conclude that the Commissioner may take part in decisions affecting the wine industry under the public generally exception.

Of course decisions regarding the WDO that target small segments of the wine industry or subsidiary businesses (like Platypus) would not meet the public generally standard. When confronted with such decisions you should seek additional advice.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

2. Decisions affecting the wine industry, specifically decisions involving ordinances of general applicability such as the Winery Definition Ordinance (WDO)?2

The July 19, 2017 Application

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any interest enumerated in Section 87103. The interests pertinent to your facts are:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in any source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official’s personal finances, including those of the official’s immediate family. This is known as the “personal financial effects” rule. (Section 87103.) However, Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if a decision also affects an official’s interest in a business entity or real property, the potential conflict of interest is analyzed under Regulation 18702.1 (relating to business interests) or Regulation 18702.2 (relating to real property interests).

Mr. Basayne has an interest in Platypus Tours pursuant to Section 87103(c) and (d). Under your facts, however, he does not have a financial interest in any of the wineries.

Thus, if a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Platypus, the Commissioner will have a conflict of interest. (Section 87103.)

1. The July 19, 2017 Application

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision or not. Regulation 18701(a) provides:

“A financial effect ... is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official’s agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).”

The decision you described does not explicitly involve the Platypus Tours. Regulation 18701(b) provides that for all other cases, if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot

be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official’s control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

The standard to determine materiality for a business entity is set forth in Regulation 18702.1.2 As applicable to your facts, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides:

“[T]he financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision’s financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity’s publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity.”

A decision to allow an existing winery to build a new tasting food facility would not appear to have a material effect on the value of Platypus.3 You stated that the applicant is but “one 125 Napa wineries included on the list on Platypus Tours’ website.” Thus, expanding one existing facility would not likely have any effect on the value of the Platypus Tours.

2. WDO Decisions

While you have not presented a specific decision for our consideration, it would appear that WDO decisions could potentially impact the entire wine industry in the County, as well as indirectly impact Platypus Tours. However, as you identified in your letter, the Act prohibits an official from making a decision only if the decision’s effect on the official’s interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Commonly referred to as the “public generally” exception. Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general test:

“A governmental decision’s financial effect on a public official’s financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment.”

Therefore, in order to invoke the exception, the official must establish that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. “Jurisdiction” is defined in Regulation 18703(d) as the jurisdiction of the state or local government agency as defined in Section 82035, or the designated geographical area the official was elected to represent, or the area to which the official’s authority and duties are limited if not elected. For Planning Commissioners, the jurisdiction at issue is the unincorporated areas of Napa County.

Regulation 18703(b) further provides that a “significant segment” of the public is at least 25 percent of:

2 Where the source of income is a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 refers back to the standard of applied in 18702.1 for business entities.

3 Regulation 18702(b) provides: “Notwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant.”

"(1) All businesses or non-profit entities within the official's jurisdiction:

"(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction: or

"(3) All individuals within the official's jurisdiction."

You stated that 816 of 2,196 businesses in unincorporated area of the county are wineries. This constitutes a significant segment of the jurisdiction (37 percent). While the Commissioner's employer is not a winery, a decision affecting the wine industry would not have a unique effect on the employer as any effect on the employer is more attenuated than an effect on the wineries. Therefore, we conclude that the Commissioner may take part in decisions affecting the wine industry under the public generally exception.

Of course decisions regarding the WDO that target small segments of the wine industry or subsidiary businesses (like Platypus) would not meet the public generally standard. When confronted with such decisions you should seek additional advice.
